project_brief.one_sentence_summary: An educational coding app for kids (6-12) that teaches Python through game-like missions.
project_brief.user_scenario: A parent wants structured coding learning for their 6-12-year-old with visible progress.
project_brief.core_problem: Kids struggle to sustain learning motivation and parents lack clear milestones.
project_brief.proposed_solution: A mission-based Python learning journey with daily progress, guided challenges, and parent-friendly dashboards.
project_brief.business_model: Subscription with tiered plans for families

constraints.timeline: MVP in 6-8 weeks; iterate after initial learning retention tests.

assumptions: Initial content can be adapted from curated educational materials.
open_questions: Which age segment shows the fastest learning retention?

research_findings: Parents are primary buyers and strongly value clear milestone visibility plus evidence that screen time converts into practical coding skills.
Children aged 6-12 churn quickly when setup is heavy or lessons are either too game-like (no real code) or too academic (too dense).
Retention improves with short mission loops, scaffolded hints, and parent-aware progress updates when learners get stuck.
Kids coding products increasingly use subscription-first pricing (roughly $5-$15/month), with retention past month two as a structural requirement.
For under-13 users, COPPA-style compliance must be explicit in PRD language: parental consent, data minimization, and deletion workflow.

resolved_answers: Parents need lightweight weekly progress summaries that show concrete learning outcomes, not just app usage.
Kids engage longer when missions are short, progressive, and provide accomplishment without giving away answers too early.
Major free coding platforms set high baseline expectations, so differentiation should focus on parent dashboard clarity and age-banded real-code learning paths.
Compliance requirements should be explicit: no behavioral ads to under-13 users, verifiable parental consent, and parent-requested data deletion workflow.
Tiered family subscriptions and annual discounting are common, but willingness-to-pay varies by segment and region.

unresolved_gaps.gap: Precise willingness-to-pay distribution across regions and age-segment households is still unclear.
unresolved_gaps.reason: Existing evidence is directional but not segmented enough for pricing decisions.

unresolved_gaps.gap: Optimal mission duration and difficulty cadence for ages 6-9 vs 10-12 needs tighter quantitative validation.
unresolved_gaps.reason: Current findings are qualitative and should be validated with product telemetry.

unresolved_gaps.gap: Cross-region compliance mapping beyond COPPA baseline (e.g., GDPR-K/UK AADC) needs implementation-level detail.
unresolved_gaps.reason: Current inputs identify obligations but not full rollout checklist by market.
